injuries.name: Ankle Injury
preventions: Maintain proper fitness; injury rates are higher for athletes that are not prepared
Use braces or athletic tape for added stability, especially for players with previous sprains
Wear basketball shoes with strong ankle support, cushioning, and traction to prevent slipping and support lateral movements

injuries.name: Foot Fracture
preventions: Wear proper footwear to reduce stress on foot and leg bones
Maintain a healthy diet to support bone strength
Maintain a healthy weight to reduce stress on bones during activity

injuries.name: Jammed Fingers
preventions: Learn correct catching and handling techniques to avoid finger injuries
Use buddy taping for extra stability and protection
Strengthen grip to improve finger resilience

injuries.name: Knee Injuries
preventions: Strengthen hamstrings, quadriceps, and calves to support knee stability
Use proper technique when jumping, pivoting, and running
Rest when experiencing knee discomfort to prevent worsening injury

injuries.name: Facial Cuts
preventions: Use a mouth guard to protect teeth and mouth
Wear safe sports glasses to protect eyes during play
Remove jewelry and avoid chewing gum during games

injuries.name: Deep Thigh Bruising
preventions: Strengthen muscles around joints to reduce injury risk
Take breaks during games or practice
Be aware of surroundings and avoid unnecessary contact

injuries.name: Overuse Injuries
preventions: Stay hydrated by drinking water regularly during and after play
Take regular breaks to avoid muscle overuse
Maintain consistent strength and conditioning training
warmup: Dynamic stretching (high knees, lunges, leg swings)

injuries.name: Head and Neck Injury
preventions: Use proper tackling technique ("heads up" football)
Complete neck-strengthening exercises
Wear helmets and any protective gear correctly

injuries.name: Shoulder Injury
preventions: Strengthen muscles surrounding the shoulder joint
Avoid tackles with a leading shoulder
Allow time for resting and recovery for shoulder joints and muscles

injuries.name: Knee Injury
preventions: Focus on hamstrings, quadriceps, and calves to support and stabilize the knees
Listen to your body and rest if there's any discomfort. Pushing through pain can lead to more serious injuries
Wear proper footwear that offers support, absorbs impact, and protects the joints

injuries.name: Ankle Injury
preventions: Incorporate ankle strengthening during gym sessions
Brace or tape ankles during high-risk activities
Choose cleats with great ankle support to prevent rolling the ankle

injuries.name: Overuse
preventions: Consistently condition year-round, even during off-season
Rest to allow muscles to repair and reduce soreness
Cross-train with other sports or exercises that target different muscle groups to prevent repetitive stress

injuries.name: Heat Injury
preventions: Constantly hydrate before, during, and after games or practices
Remove your football gear during breaks to allow body heat to escape
Avoid jumping from cool environments straight into intense, full-pad training; gradually increase intensity in the heat

injuries.name: Ankle Sprain
preventions: Maintain proper fitness; injury rates are higher for athletes that are not prepared
Use braces or athletic tape for added stability, especially for players with previous sprains
Wear court shoes with strong ankle support, cushioning, and traction to support lateral movements

injuries.name: ACL Injury
preventions: Use proper jumping and landing techniques and avoid straightening the knees on landing
Strengthen the quadriceps, hamstrings, and glutes to provide stability to the knee
Run prevention warm-up routines at least 3x a week before, during, and after season
warmup: Box jumps, vertical jumps, and lateral movements

injuries.name: Shoulder Injury
preventions: Focus on exercises that strengthen the muscles around the shoulder joint
Work with coaches to ensure your hitting mechanics are correct
Shoulder injuries are often repetitive — rest between games or training sessions

injuries.name: Finger Injury
preventions: Tape an injured or vulnerable finger to a healthy one for extra stability
Strengthen your grip with proper exercises to prevent finger injuries
Maintain good physical condition so muscles and tendons can handle impact

injuries.name: Head Injury
preventions: Clearly call "mine" or "I got it" when the ball is in the air to avoid teammate collisions
Watch for balls coming from different courts, especially during warm-ups
Never turn your back to the net during warm-ups or active play to avoid being hit by unexpected serves or hits

injuries.name: Overuse
preventions: Work with coaches to ensure proper form when hitting, serving, and blocking to avoid overstraining joints
Take 1-2 days off per week to allow muscles and joints to repair
Wear protective and supportive shoes to alleviate knee and ankle strain
warmup: Light cardio (jogging, high knees, butt kicks)
